entry.title: VIM 7.5 ECC – MRKO Posting Does Not Finalize DP Workflow (BUS_OBJTYPE / BUS_OBJKEY Missing)
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I’m currently working on an issue in SAP ECC with OpenText VIM 7.5, related to MRKO posting and VIM Document Processing (DP) workflow finalization, and I’m looking for guidance or best practices from anyone who has faced a similar scenario.

System Context

  • SAP ECC
  • OpenText VIM 7.5
  • Invoice processing uses VIM DP with workflow
  • Posting is done via MRKO (consignment settlement) rather than MIRO

Current Design

  • The VIM workflow starts correctly.
  • The posting step uses a custom copy of /OPT/VIM_52_BAPI_DOCLINES_PO.
  • Inside this copy, MRKO is executed via SUBMIT.
  • MRKO successfully creates the FI document (BELNR / BUKRS / GJAHR).

Issue / Symptoms

After the FI document is created:

  • The workflow remains active in SWIA and is not completed.
  • VIM Analytics loses the workflow association.
  • In the workflow container, BUS_OBJTYPE and BUS_OBJKEY are empty.

Root Cause (Analysis So Far)

From our investigation, the problem does not seem to be MRKO itself, but how it is executed:

  • MRKO is triggered outside of VIM’s standard DP posting framework.
  • As a result, DP is never finalized.
  • VIM is not informed of the created FI document.
  • BOR linkage is missing, so the workflow cannot bind to the posted document and close properly.

Has anyone come across this situation when using MRKO with VIM DP? What is the recommended or supported way to correctly finalize DP and close the workflow in this scenario? Are there specific VIM classes or methods that should be used to link the FI document back to the DP document? Any advice or experience would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you!

entry.title: Re: Designing Goods Receipt Strategies in SAP S/4HANA:An End-to-End Analysis of 101 and 107/109 Move
entry.content:
Hi @mauriciocastiblanco 

My updates are as follows:

#1: Purchase order creation: In this document, are the plant and storage location entered EWM?

Reply: Yes

#2#3: Executed 107 movement: Is this goods receipt posting to an EWM plant/storage location or a non-EWM storage location?
When executing movement 107, in which storage location is the stock temporarily stored?

Reply: 107 movement only have the plant, which says material is in transit. It does not include storage location. after 109 only, physical material will be received at storage location level.

Please let me know, if you have any question futrther.

entry.title: Delivery partner details from CDS not released for development
entry.content:
The CDS views to fetch the delivery partner details such as I_DeliveryDocumentPartner, I_DeliveryPartnerAddress, I_OutboundDeliveryPartnerTP are not released to use for development.

Please provide any accessible CDS views for the same.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Delivery partner details from CDS not released for de...
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

Thank you for your inquiry regarding the use of CDS views for retrieving delivery partner details.

We would like to inform you that the CDS views I_DeliveryDocumentPartner, I_DeliveryPartnerAddress, and I_OutboundDeliveryPartnerTP are currently not released for use in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition extensibility scenarios.

As per SAP’s extensibility guidelines, only released CDS views and APIs can be used for custom development. Unreleased objects are restricted and may change without notice.

We recommended the following approach to achieve your requirement using supported artifacts, see options below:

Option 1 – Using Released CDS Views

You can derive delivery partner information by combining:

I_DeliveryDocument (to obtain partner roles such as Ship-To / Sold-To)
I_BusinessPartner
I_BusPartAddress
I_Address_2

This approach allows you to retrieve business partner and address details in a supported manner.

Option 2 – Using Official API (Recommended for Partner Data)

Alternatively, you can use the released API:

Service: API_OUTBOUND_DELIVERY_SRV
Entity: A_OutbDeliveryPartner

This API provides direct access to outbound delivery partner data and is the closest supported alternative to the unreleased CDS views.

Important nota that currently, there is no single released CDS view that fully replaces the functionality of the unreleased delivery partner CDS views mentioned above. Therefore, a combination of released CDS views or the use of the official API is required.

We hope this clarifies the situation. 

In case this CDS views are critical for your business, you are encouraged to create an improvement request. SAP Customer Influence enables customers to submit improvement requests and to openly vote on them.

3206294 - How to Submit Feature Requests for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Enterprise Portfolio and Project Management
https://me.sap.com/notes/3206294/en-US 

Regards,
Leticia
SAP Technical Support

entry.title: Why SAP Databricks Genie Is a Game‑Changer: Key Benefits and Expert Tips
entry.content:
SAP Databricks Genie Code is an AI-powered assistant inside Databricks that helps you generate, optimize, explain, and fix code or queries directly in notebooks, SQL editors, dashboards, and jobs. It works both as a conversational helper and in “Agent mode” where it can autonomously handle multi-step tasks.

To open Genie Code pane, click on the upper-right corner of the page. 

How Genie Code Works

  • Context-aware assistant: Genie Code understands the environment you’re working in (SQL editor, notebook, file editor, etc.) and tailors its suggestions accordingly.
  • Conversational interface: You can ask questions in plain English, and Genie Code translates them into SQL or Python queries.
  • Agent mode: Beyond single queries, Genie Code can autonomously execute multi-step workflows, such as preparing data, running transformations, and generating reports.
  • Integration with Databricks-hosted AI models: By default, Genie Code uses Databricks’ own models, but you can enable partner-powered AI features for extended capabilities

Key Benefits

  • No-code analytics: Genie Code allows business users to query data without writing SQL, making Databricks more accessible.
  • Faster development: Developers can generate boilerplate code, optimize queries, and debug issues quickly.
  • Self-service BI: Teams can ask natural language questions and instantly get insights, charts, or summaries.
  • Error reduction: Genie Code explains code snippets, helping users understand logic and avoid mistakes.

Quick Comparison: Genie Code vs. Manual Coding

Feature 
Genie Code Manual Coding
Query generationNatural language → SQL/PythonRequires SQL/Python expertise
Error fixingAutomated suggestionsManual debugging
Workflow automationAgent mode handles multi-step tasksMust script everything
AccessibilityBusiness users can query dataLimited to technical users
SpeedInstant insightsSlower, depends on coding skill

Type questions or slash commands  in the text box at the bottom of Genie Code pane and press Enter or click

 Short Demo of SAP Incentive Management - Agent Mode for TCMP Table for CS_Payments table via (MCP) for faster SQL results

How Genie Code Works with MCP

  • Agent Mode only: MCP servers are supported exclusively when Genie Code runs in Agent mode.
  • Workspace integration: Genie Code can use any MCP servers added to your Databricks workspace, provided you have permission.
  • Tool exposure: MCP servers expose tools, datasets, and workflows to Genie Code, enabling it to execute tasks like:
    • Running ETL pipelines
    • Querying external APIs
    • Automating reporting workflows
  • Dynamic context retrieval: Instead of hardcoding instructions, Genie Code fetches relevant context from MCP servers in real time.

Genie Code pane controls and settings for MCP, Instructions and Skills

Databricks Genie integrates with MCP (Model Context Protocol) to connect AI agents with external tools, workflows, and contextual data. This allows Genie Code to go beyond simple query generation and act as a true agent that can access structured resources in your workspace.

entry.title: Temporary Protected Status Extension
entry.content:
There have been a few notices from E-Verify lately regarding the extension of Temporary Protected Statuses based on official court rulings. As an example, it instructs "when completing the Expiration Date (if any) fields on Form I-9, input “as per court order” in Section 1 and “April 15, 2026” in Section 2 along with a note in the additional information box." 

Currently, Section 1 and Section 2 of the I-9 in SuccessFactors only allows for a date to be entered in the expiration date field. 

Anyone know a workaround, have a solution to enter the text on Form I-9 in Section 1 or Section 2, or received any guidance from SAP Support on how to account for these scenarios?

entry.title: 🇧🇷 ABAP AI: Capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers CodeJam (Campinas, Brazil)
entry.content:
In this CodeJam session, you will learn how to use ABAP capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers, including predictive code completion, unit test generation for ABAP CDS and ABAP code, and code explanation to speed up your development with ABAP Cloud.

This is an in-person event only (not virtual) and is planned for Saturday 11th April 2026 in Campinas, Brazil from 13:15 - 17:15 local time. The language and content of the event will be in English.

Location:

Event Address: FACAMP Faculdades de Campinas - Avenida Alan Turing - Cidade Universitária, Campinas - SP, Brasil

** IMPORTANT ** Only participants of the SAP INSIDE TRACK CAMPINAS who are registered HERE are allowed to attend!

Please note that spaces are limited - we can only accommodate 30 attendees. If you wish to attend, register by clicking the Yes button in the Will you be attending? section above to the right.

The instructors for this event are the Developer Advocates @thomas_jung and @RichHeilman 

There are essential prerequisites that must be completed by every attendee, before arriving at the event location. They are documented here: abap-exercises-codejam/prerequisites.md at main · SAP-samples/abap-exercises-codejam (github.com).

For more information on SAP CodeJam events, to ask questions, and to chat with your fellow attendees, head over to the SAP CodeJam group.

entry.title: 🇧🇷 Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core (Campinas, Brazil)
entry.content:
This SAP CodeJam event is on the topic of Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core and Python. You do not need to have any background in the topic, but a lot of curiosity!

In this CodeJam you will learn how to use Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core to implement a retrieval augmented generation (RAG) use case to improve the responses of large language models (LLMs) and reduce hallucinations. You will learn how to deploy an LLM and the orchestration service on SAP AI Core and query it via SAP AI Launchpad and the Python SDK. Furthermore, you will learn about the most important genAI concepts and create and use embeddings to improve your RAG response and build your own chatbot with memory.

Prerequisites: Bring your own laptop with power adapter and have a supported Web browser such as Google Chrome or Firefox installed on your laptop.

This is an in-person event only (not virtual) and is planned for Saturday 11th April 2026 in Campinas, Brazil from 13:15 - 17:15 local time. The language and content of the event will be in English.

Location:

Event Address: FACAMP Faculdades de Campinas - Avenida Alan Turing - Cidade Universitária, Campinas - SP, Brasil

** IMPORTANT ** Only participants of the SAP INSIDE TRACK CAMPINAS who are registered HERE are allowed to attend!

Please note that spaces are limited - we can only accommodate 30 attendees. If you wish to attend, register by clicking the Yes button in the Will you be attending? section above to the right.

The instructors for this event are the Developer Advocates @Vitaliy-R and @noravonthenen

entry.title: CNPJ Alfanumérico – Fase 2 Concluída!
entry.content:
Boa tarde pessoal,

Dando seguimento ao planejamento das mudanças do CNPJ, comunicamos com satisfação a conclusão de todas as entregas da Fase 2 do projeto CNPJ Alfanumérico no módulo HCM. Este marco reforça nosso compromisso com a conformidade e aderência às exigências legais estabelecidas pela Reforma Tributária.

Se você perdeu a atualização anterior sobre a Fase 1 ou deseja relembrar os detalhes das mudanças iniciais, confira o nosso blogpost anterior:
👉 Acesse o Blogpost da Fase 1 aqui

O que representa essa entrega?

Com a Fase 2 concluída, o módulo HCM passa a estar compatível com o novo formato de CNPJ alfanumérico, tanto para cadastro quanto para processamento das informações. Essa evolução é fundamental para o atendimento à Reforma Tributária brasileira e assegura conformidade legal para os próximos anos.

Importante:
Lembramos que a adoção do CNPJ alfanumérico pode envolver outros módulos SAP, além do HCM. Consulte sempre a SAP Leading Note 3714871 para atualizações de toda a solução.

Principais entregas desta fase

SAP Notes liberadas na Fase 2:

  • 3730575 - Prerequisite Objects for SAP Note 3720943, 3732954 and 3730656
  • 3720943 - Alphanumeric CNPJ - Adapt Empregos simultâneos infotype (0437)
  • 3732954 - Prerequisite Objects for SAP Note 3730656
  • 3730656 - HR Renewal - Alphanumeric CNPJ - Adapt Empregos simultâneos infotype (0437)
  • 3728988 - Prerequisite objects for SAP Notes 3726057 and 3731328
  • 3726057 - Alphanumeric CNPJ - Implementation for HCMBR Reports Phase 2
  • 3728895 - Prerequisite objects for SAP Note 3731328
  • 3731328 - eSocial - Alphanumeric CNPJ - Changes for event S-2230
  • 3730625 - Alphanumeric CNPJ - Information of Historical ABAP Objects

Principais mudanças realizadas:

  • Adequação completa do Infotipo Empregos Simultâneos (0437) para suportar o novo formato de CNPJ
  • Atualizações de relatórios legais e eventos do eSocial para o padrão alfanumérico
  • Ajustes finais em tabelas, domínios, validações e integrações relevantes

 Gostou deste conteúdo?

Deixe seu like, compartilhe com colegas e registre dúvidas, experiências ou sugestões nos comentários!
Siga a tag HCM Payroll Brazil na SAP Community para se manter sempre atualizado.

Um grande abraço!

Anderson Marques

entry.title: Inside SAP Build at SAP Sapphire 2026: What to Expect from AI Agents and Agentic Development
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire is almost here, and SAP Build at Sapphire is where customers and partners will be able to see how to easily create and deploy AI agents starting with simple language descriptions.  

For anyone tracking where AI agents and agentic development are headed in the enterprise, SAP Build will offer a chance to see it up close. Attendees will be able to experience how SAP Build helps teams build AI agents across SAP. This includes hands-on experience, live demos, sessions, and direct access to the teams building these capabilities. 

This year, the focus is on making agentic development real - moving from intent to working solution through a guided, end-to-end experience. 

 The hands-on experience: Build an agent with Joule Studio 

The biggest draw for SAP Build at Sapphire will be the hands-on experience on the show floor. 

Attendees can sit down at an open station and work through guided scenarios in a live system – building agents with Joule Studio, generating app logic from intent, and integrating with SAP data and workflows. This is a practical view at how agentic development works from start to deployment. 

This experience builds on the popular SAP Build Developer Garage at TechEd, which became a go-to stop for developers who wanted to get hands-on and see SAP Build in action. At Sapphire, we are bringing that energy back and making it bigger for a broader audience. 

Space will be limited, and access will be first-come, first-serve, so this is one stop worth making early. 

The sessions 

SAP Build sessions at Sapphire will focus on how teams are building AI agents, clean-core extensions and automation workflows. You’ll see how agentic development is evolving - from defining intent to generating and deploying working solutions - and how Joule is integrated across the development lifecycle. 

For anyone trying to separate AI hype from AI execution, these sessions are worth prioritizing. 

The booth 

The SAP Build and Agentic AI booths are where live walk-throughs and real conversations happen. 

Bring your own use cases, connect directly with SAP experts, and validate how SAP Build fits into your architecture. Whether you are building your first extension or scaling AI-driven workflows, the booth’s are designed for both strategic and practical discussions.  

Expect demos, conversations, and a chance to get closer to what SAP Build is showing at Sapphire. 

 

Why stop by SAP Build at SAP Sapphire 

AI agents are a major focus at Sapphire but SAP Build is where you can see how they are actually built and deployed in SAP environments. 

Get hands-on. Talk to the experts. See how agentic development works in practice. 

Plan your SAP Build stop at SAP Sapphire: 

  • Register for SAP Sapphire 
  • Browse SAP Build sessions 
  • Find the SAP Build booth (see the map onsite)

entry.title: Q1 2026: How’d it go for you?
entry.content:
When Plans Meet Reality, Resilience Becomes the Differentiator

Here we are at the end of Q1.  Did Q1 run the way you thought it would? 

Back in December, many supply chain and logistics teams entered 2026 with cautious expectations. Capacity didn’t look especially tight overall. Rates seemed to appear manageable in certain areas. Planning cycles felt, if not calm, maybe a bit more familiar than in previous years.

And yet, to say the least, Q1 probably didn’t unfold the way you thought it would.

Volatility didn’t ebb and flow. It spiked, and it stuck around. Geopolitical instability, trade friction, route disruption, energy price swings, and capacity uncertainty overlapped and compounded — forcing teams to re‑plan continuously based information that was more than challenging to pin down. 

So, if this quarter felt harder than expected on your logistics operations, you’re not alone.

When disruption stops being episodic

If you have not sensed this yet, Q1 was an effective reminder — disruption is no longer episodic, it’s structural.

In March alone, escalating conflict in the Middle East sharply reduced shipping activity through the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world’s most critical maritime chokepoints. Major carriers suspended or diverted services, war‑risk surcharges were introduced, and transit times stretched by weeks as vessels rerouted around southern Africa.

At the same time, Red Sea security risks, tariff uncertainty, and fluctuating energy costs amplified pressure across global trade lanes, turning what looked like available capacity on paper into unreliable capacity in practice.

In Q1, many organizations realized they are no longer operating quarter to quarter — they are operating condition to condition.

Reframing success: Resilience is the new efficiency

Remember when efficiency was the benchmark? Optimize networks. Minimize cost. Squeeze idle time out of execution.

Q1 was just the latest challenge to that axiom.

Efficiency assumes repeatability. Resilience assumes change.
Efficiency optimizes the plan. Resilience protects execution when the plan breaks.

In early 2026, the teams pulling through were not the ones with the most optimized spreadsheets — they were the ones that maintained visibility, agility, and alignment.  They were able to be incisive and decisive as conditions changed around them.

Resilience is no longer aspirational — it’s the new benchmark.

Losing certainty before losing cargo

One of the destabilizing problems in Q1 wasn’t lost shipments — it was lost certainty.

Teams weren’t asking, “Where is my freight?”
They were asking:

  • Is this delay temporary or systemic?
  • Is the shipment late — or blocked?
  • Do we intervene now, or wait?

In volatile corridors, poor signal quality creates overreaction. Everything feels urgent because context is missing. And without context, execution decisions become educated guesses.  

This is where the Q1 innovations in SAP Business Network for Logistics matter — not because they “add visibility,” but because they improve the quality of execution signals.

Across Q1, solution updates expanded event semantics and milestone context:

  • Differentiating readiness, holds, and execution deviations through new event types
  • Providing clearer execution context through expanded event reason codes
  • Extending visibility across modes, including rail, and deeper, into container‑level tracking

The result is not simply knowing what happened, but understanding why it happened, earlier — when response options still exist.

When capacity exists but can’t be used reliably

Another common Q1 experience: capacity that exists, but cannot be used with confidence.

On paper, many markets looked loose. In practice, docks, yards, and terminals became friction points. Overbooking, manual scheduling workarounds, and local constraints eroded predictability quickly — and small breakdowns cascaded across schedules.

In this environment, the plan often didn’t fail. Capacity did.

Q1 reinforced the importance of enforcing reality at execution time. Innovations in SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration strengthened dock appointment and capacity control:

  • Recurring and maximum‑capacity blocks at loading points
  • Clear enforcement of booking rules across shipper and carrier apps
  • Automatic validation of appointments via APIs, with deterministic confirmation or rejection

These capabilities don’t create more capacity — they protect what must not break. They replace informal coordination with shared rules, giving teams predictable throughput even when volumes and routes shift.

When exceptions become everyday work

Q1 also reminded teams that exceptions are no longer exceptional.

Rerouting exposes shipments to new compliance rules, documentation requirements, safety constraints, and handling risks — especially for regulated or dangerous goods. Under pressure, ambiguity is costly. Misinterpreting a handling instruction or compliance hold can mean days lost instead of hours.  

Q1 enhancements improved how execution context is shared across partners:

  • Clearer classification of dangerous‑goods information
  • Distinct separation between hazard identification and handling instructions
  • Richer event context shared between shippers and carriers
  • More detailed delivery instructions for complex delivery sites

This reduces back‑and‑forth, lowers risk, and allows teams to focus less on interpretation and more on action.

What Q1 changed — not as features, but as behaviors

Taken together, Q1 offered a lot to enable behavioral change:

  • From reacting late → to intervening earlier
  • From informal coordination → to enforceable execution rules
  • From fragile plans → to adaptive, partner‑ready collaboration

SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration and SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace remain complementary solutions — but in Q1, execution across them became even more streamlined and consistent. Events, milestones, and execution states align more clearly across planning, execution, and visibility, reducing operational blind spots and strengthening day‑to‑day execution under pressure.

And for teams running SAP Logistics Management, Q1 also lowered the friction between planning changes and execution — with simpler, out‑of‑the‑box connectivity into SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration when tenders, subcontracting, or carrier changes were needed quickly.

This matters because resilience is not grounded in one place. It’s built when execution comes together with visibility across systems and partners to overcome stress and uncertainty.

Pulling through is what sets you apart

Q1 didn’t reward perfect forecasting. It rewarded adaptability.

The organizations that stood out weren’t immune to disruption. They pulled through because they maintained visibility, dealt with execution reality, and stayed aligned with partners as conditions changed.

We never stop changing, evolving, or improving — because the world around supply chains doesn’t stop either.

Read more about Q1 innovations in our monthly Community posts: 
January – 2601
February – 2602
March – 2603

And get the technical details from BN4L’s What’s New page on Help.SAP.com.  

Learn more about SAP Business Network for Logistics and SAP’s suite of supply chain logistics solutions.

entry.title: SAP Functional take on the real bottleneck - Who will train the bots?
entry.content:
If you work as an SAP functional consultant and also have a broader interest in technology, systems, and how things connect across domains, this perspective may be relevant to you. Read on.
SAP AI core provisioned us to BYOM(Bring your own model) concept. But to take advantage of local(own model), context rich, scalable, cost effective( remember last API token bill!) model, we also need to cleanse data, plot data and train the model. This blog discusses about feature engineering and mathematics behind feature selection in training ANN( artificial neural network) models using
Tensor flow playground as an example.

Prelude : There is a popular narrative forming. America builds the large language models.
China builds the robots. But this framing misses the harder problem. Someone still has to train these systems for real work.

Business case: A hospital chain in Ireland wants to replace human operators who answer incoming calls. The idea sounds straightforward: feed 10 years of call recordings into an AI system and automate the job. In reality, this is where the real effort begins.

Raw data is messy. / Call logs are inconsistent. / Audio quality varies. / Context is missing.
Sensitive information needs to be handled carefully.

Before any model training happens, there is a long pipeline:

* Cleaning and filtering unusable data
* Transcribing audio accurately
* Structuring conversations into usable formats
* Labelling intent, urgency, and outcomes
* Handling compliance and privacy constraints

Even then, training is not a one-time task.
The system needs continuous feedback, correction, and retraining to match real-world expectations.


Few notes if you are a polymath:

Tensor flow is machine learning framework provided by Google. A colleague challenged me to train a model on a given dataset with a clear objective: achieve the lowest possible test loss using the most efficient number of epochs.

Practical use case -

• Credit risk scoring

• Sales forecasting

Training Process Parameters

Epoch

  • What it is: One complete forward and backward pass of all training data through the network.

  • When to use: Tune to control training duration. Stop when validation loss stops improving (Early Stopping).

  • How to remember: Reading a textbook. 1 Epoch = reading the entire book cover-to-cover once.

Learning Rate (e.g., 0.03)

  • What it is: The step size the optimizer takes to update weights during gradient descent.

  • When to use: Adjust if training fails. High LR = fast but unstable (loss spikes). Low LR = stable but slow (gets stuck). 0.03 is a standard, moderate starting point.

  • How to remember: Mini-golf putt. High LR is a full swing (overshoots the hole). Low LR is a gentle tap (takes forever to reach).

Batch Size (e.g., 10)

  • What it is: The number of data samples processed before the model updates its internal weights.

  • When to use: Smaller batches (e.g., 10) add noise to the learning path, helping escape local minima but computing slower. Larger batches compute faster but require more memory.

  • How to remember: Carrying groceries. Do you update your trip carrying 1 item at a time, 10 items (mini-batch), or all at once (full batch)?

Model Architecture

Activation (e.g., Tanh)

  • What it is: A mathematical function attached to a neuron that decides whether it should "fire," introducing non-linearity to learn complex patterns.

  • When to use: Tanh is useful for hidden layers when zero-centered outputs are needed. ReLU is the modern default for deep networks.

  • How to remember: A club bouncer. Determines who gets to enter inside.(next layer pass filter)

Hidden Layers

  • What it is: The layers of mathematical operations between input features and final output. They extract increasingly complex patterns.

  • When to use: Add more layers/neurons when the model cannot capture the complexity of the data (underfitting). Reduce them if the model memorizes data (overfitting).

  • How to remember: An assembly line. Layer 1 detects edges, Layer 2 combines edges into shapes, Layer 3 identifies the object.

Regularization (L1 / L2)

  • What it is: A penalty added to the loss function to discourage the model from learning overly complex, extreme weights (prevents overfitting).

    • When to use: Apply when training loss is very low, but test loss is high.

    • How to remember: A complexity tax. Forces the model to find the simplest possible rule to separate the data rather than drawing crazy, intricate boundaries.

    Regularization Rate

    • What it is: The hyperparameter controlling the strength of the regularization penalty.

    • When to use: Tune alongside Regularization. Start small (0.001). Too high = model learns nothing.

    • How to remember: The specific tax percentage.

      Data & Evaluation

      Data

      • What it is: The underlying pattern or distribution of the raw dataset (e.g., circles, spirals, XOR).

      • When to use: Select different data shapes to test how well a specific architecture handles non-linear boundaries.

      Ratio of training to test data (e.g., 50%)

      • What it is: The split between data used to update weights (Train) and data held back to evaluate generalization (Test).

      • When to use: 80/20 is enterprise standard. 50/50 is used in TF Playground to artificially limit training data, forcing the model to work harder and highlighting overfitting quickly.

      • How to remember: Practice exams vs. Final Exam.

      Noise (e.g., 10)

      • What it is: Random variance or incorrect labels injected into the dataset.

      • When to use: Increase noise to simulate messy, real-world data and test model robustness.

      • How to remember: Listening to someone in a crowded room. Can the model find the signal despite the static?

      Features

      • What it is: The specific input representations     fed into the first layer.

      • When to use: Use feature engineering to help a simple network solve a complex geometric problem without adding hidden layers.

      • How to remember: House price prediction variables (Square footage, ZIP code, Age).

      Output: Test loss / Training loss

      • What it is: A numerical metric of how wrong the model's predictions are. Training loss evaluates seen data; Test loss evaluates unseen data.

      • Formula (Mean Squared Error):     Loss                        

      • When to use: Monitor continuously. If Training loss drops but Test loss rises, stop training (overfitting).

      • How to remember: Training loss = score on homework. Test loss = score on the final exam.

      Discretize Output

      • What it is: Forcing the continuous probability map (soft gradients) into rigid classification boundaries (solid colors).

      • When to use: Enable when you need to see the exact decision boundary the model has drawn, rather than its confidence levels.

      • How to remember: Rounding a grade. 89.5% becomes a discrete "A" rather than a continuous decimal.

Feature engineering -Mathematics behind model training- my learning!

  • A neural network is a computational model inspired by the human brain that consists of interconnected nodes, called neurons, which are organized in layers. The nodes are connected to the nodes in the next layer, creating a network. The first layer is the input layer, followed by hidden layers, and the last layer is the output layer.

Challenge 1-  Circular data set & a classification problem type- Target- achieve Test loss less eq. 0.003

( Refer notes above -that is more important than training loss)- landing page - before model training setup change

Go to below url to try it out yourselves. STOP reading if you want to solve yourself since you now understand each parameter !

A Neural Network Playground

How to solve?

The key is to understand the dataset first, and then tune the model accordingly.

For example, when working with a circular dataset, feature selection should be guided by the underlying geometry. We must remember formula of circle x2   + y2    = r 2

Hence in below solution feature introduced accordingly, activation Tanh used for centered output. Fine tune learning rate, Regularization and Reg. Rate. Important note ratio of training to test data chosen as 70%,batch size 15- however you could try with increasing noise in the dataset and yet tune the model to achieve similar result. Minimum Epoch count is desired and we strive to avoid 'overfitting' and under fitting of training data to the model. Final setup below-

Challenge 2-Spiral data set & a classification problem type- Target- achieve Test loss less eq. 0.003
Note- If I keep above settings for training, model does not work at all- the Epocs( training run time) rises but 'Test loss' does not reduce.
How to solve- analysis and inference

Retail context

  • Circle dataset represent = Purely Seasonal (same every year).
  • Spiral dataset represent = Seasonal + Growth/Trend.

Following similar analogy of circular data set. I have now solved spiral dataset as well.
It is tied with mathematics again.

Unlike circular dataset ,here formula is not x2   + y2    = r 2

a Spiral is defined by a radius that changes in proportion to the angle 

Next the main trick ,for circular dataset, I was  using below features

These features only tell the model "how far" a point is from the centre, but they completely ignore the angle. For a spiral, two points can have the same distance from the centre but belong to different arms because their angles are different.

To solve the spiral efficiently (low epoch count), you need features that help the model understand Rotation.

So we shall choose Sin(x) function to add the periodicity (repeating) flavour.

If the Playground allowed it, the ideal feature would be

but since it doesn't, we  rely on increased network depth. I have used gradual decreasing neurons per layer.(Tapered approach, so that model generalise to new data)

Final setup shown below-

Next - You could try to increase Noise parameter to 10 to simulate dirty data common in SAP system.
also could try another round of optimisation minimalist architect's approach by reducing hidden layer to 3 and increasing Neuron count slightly in the middle.

Challenge 3- Gaussian pattern dataset& a classification problem type-

Target- achieve Test loss less eq. 0.001

How to solve - Setup 1

Setup 2(Note Epoch increased compared to 1st trial)

 

Setup-3 - Note Test loss is zero in each case but Epoch is still not optimised!

Maths Alert! - we might ask - Why X1 and X2 feature are sufficient for training gaussian data set where mathematically it looks for mean value and complex formula?

Being pragmatic- Linear separability: The Gaussian dataset shown consists of two distinct clusters. Because they are separated in space, a simple straight line is enough to divide the orange class from the blue class.
Hence,X1 and X2 are sufficient because the neural network is performing binary classification, not density estimation. It does not need to learn or replicate the complex Gaussian probability formula; it only needs to draw a straight line between the two clusters.

Experiment never stopped!

Setup 4 - FINAL -note BATCH SIZE = 1

This is called "Stochastic Gradient Descent." The model will update its weights after every single run, allowing it to find the boundary line much faster in terms of epochs.

 

Real world Stress test - Next trial - increase the noise to 30 and see the model behaviour

 

Challenge 4- XOR dataset & a classification problem type

Final setup : Note no hidden layer(neurons) yet target of Training loss achieved.

Maths rationale:  

The classic XOR dataset consists of four quadrants that cannot be separated by drawing a single straight line using raw X1 and X2 inputs. By using the product X1 X2 as the sole feature, the math resolves the problem before training even begins:

  • Top-Right Quadrant: X1 is (+), X2 is (+)  --> (+ x +) = Positive

  • Bottom-Left Quadrant: X1  is (-), X2 is (-) -->  (- x -) = Positive

  • Top-Left Quadrant: X1 is (-), X2 is (+) --> (- x +) = Negative

  • Bottom-Right Quadrant: X1 is (+), X2 is (-)  -->  (+ x -) = Negative


Food for thought : In modern enterprise AI systems, hand-crafting polynomial interactions across thousands of features is impossible. It is a bad idea to design architectures relying on this. To build scalable models, you must pass raw features into hidden layers with non-linear activations (like ReLU) so the network automatically learns these complex cross-feature interactions.

Conclusion: (Pls ignore formatting issues )Try out the fun yourself!  A Neural Network Playground

Left- Regression problems for you! - We try to predict a continuously changing output - it is not categorical also read more about:  1/ SGD (Stochastic Gradient Descent) 2/ ADAM (Adaptive Moment Estimation) as efficient optimization algorithm.

entry.title: Multistage advanced intercompany sales and stock transfer with S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 release
entry.content:
Most of the large organizations run SAP and more than 75% of world’s transaction happen in SAP. Many of these organizations running SAP have their plants or operations across several countries. It is very common that the company unit in Germany (DE) takes the order for a product from a German customer, but they do not produce in Germany and hence brings the product from their United states (US) plant and delivers the product to the customer.

SAP supports these scenarios for several years with two process flows

  1. Inter-company sales
  2. Inter-company stock transfer order

However, these classic intercompany sales have some limitations . SAP released single stage Advanced intercompany (AIC) sales and stock transfer between 2 entities with S/4HANA 2022 release eliminating those limitations  of classic intercompany sales. Refer my earlier blog post https://www.linkedin.com/posts/mrinalkroy_activity-7021224794213761024-paiE?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAACqeFcBFLp4MZaIFhaLzuKFWx73dXzaU2U to know more about the limitations of classic intercompany sales and single stage advanced intercompany sales and stock transfer.

SAP has released multistage advanced intercompany sales and stock transfer with S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 release which will be game changer for intercompany sales for organizations running on SAP. Most of the SAP customers were looking for multistage  feature of AIC to take care of tax , revenue sharing etc. Physical flow of goods may happen from delivering plant of the delivering company to the customer in the selling company, but one or multiple affiliates other than the delivering company and selling company may be involved in these intercompany transactions.

A high-level process flow for multistage advanced intercompany sales with selling company 1610 in Sweden (SE) , intermediate company 1010 in Germany (DE) and delivering company 1810 in United states (US) is depicted in below image.

Presence of

  • transit plant in shipping tab at the sales order line item is interpreted as advanced intercompany sales order
  • financial chain in shipping tab at the sales order line item is interpreted as multistage advanced intercompany sales order

Transit plant is 1010 DE and financial chain ID 9000000002 (1810(US)_1010(DE)_1610(SE)) in the sales order 10005795 are depicted below.

Financial chain ID 9000000002 with Fiori app Manage financial chain shows

  • Delivering plant as 1810 US
  • Intermediate or transit plant as 1010 DE
  • Selling plant as 1610 SE

Once the sales order (1) 10005795 is created , the following documents  are created automatically by VCM (Value chain monitor) .

(2) Supplying company purchase order 4500003607 at SE with intercompany supplier as DE

(3) Intercompany sales order 60001367 at DE with Sold to party as SE

(4) Intermediate company purchase order 4500003608 at DE with intercompany supplier as US

(5) Intercompany sales order 60001368 at US with Sold to party as DE

Outbound delivery (6) is in pending status .

VCM is the backbone of advanced intercompany sales and stock transfer . It supports orchestration and monitoring for end-to-end process flows, executes process steps automatically, depicts dependency of documents (predecessor or successor) in relevant process steps, tracks process steps, detects defects, helps to analyze errors, reprocesses the steps after fixing the error, and checks completeness of individual documents and overall flow.

Once the outbound delivery is created from the sales order and post goods issue (PGI) is executed the ‘Reload’ of Fiori app Monitor value chains shows the process flow as below. Process step DC-GT (7) is executed with stock transfer to valuated stock-in-transit (VSiT). Movement type is 687 (GMDel to Iss Val SiT).

Internal transfer of control dates under shipping tab of outbound delivery are new fields for AIC sales , those have been populated as below manually for the respective sales organizations .

And subsequently following goods movements happen automatically by VCM .

(8) DC-GI (Delivering company) Goods Issue with movement type 687

(9) IC-GR (Intermediate company Goods Receipt) with movement type 101

(10) IC-GI ( Intermediate company Goods Issue) with movement type 601

(11) SC-GR (Selling company Goods Receipt) with movement type 101

Transfer of ownership from the selling company to the external customer is done by populating the date / time in external transfer of control date in outbound delivery.

The last step of goods movement SC-GI (Selling company Goods Issue ) is then executed with movement type 601.

Subsequently , intercompany invoices , customer invoice and supplier invoices are created and the app Monitor value chains shows the process flow as completed with all the documents .

Like to highlight the following

  • There is only one outbound delivery
  • Physical flow of goods happen directly from delivering plant to the external customer
  • Transit plant is only for goods movements from system perspectives with relevant title of ownership
  • Location substituin is possible with Advanced Available-to-promise
  • Process flow can be executed with Extended warehouse management (EWM) at delivering plant
  • Process flow can be executed with Transportation management (TM) with relevant incoterms and freight cost distribution in relevant companies
  • Internal and external transfer of control dates can be populated automatically with configurations , BAdI with business rules / incoterms , Transportation management TM events etc.
  • AIC supports valuated stock-in-transit with relevant transfer of ownerships
  • AIC supports event based revenue recognition (EBRR) , Trade compliance , Product compliance

A completed process flow for multistage advanced intercompany stock transfer is also depicted below.

This blog is based on my personal tests , observations , insights in S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 release. Will appreciate your feedback / comments.

entry.title: Re: Delivery partner details from CDS not released for de...
entry.content: These CDS views are not released for custom development use. The supported option is to use the released Outbound Delivery API API_OUTBOUND_DELIVERY_SRV, especially A_OutbDeliveryPartner and A_OutbDeliveryAddress for partner and address data. If you also need Business Partner master/address details, use the released Business Partner API API_BUSINESS_PARTNER.

entry.title: Re: What business trend is currently having the biggest i...
entry.content:
This is a very subjective question. For the most part, there is a constant stream of “next big things,” and a lot of them sound transformative in presentations but barely move the needle in day-to-day work. The real test is simple: does it change how decisions get made, how fast work moves, or how teams collaborate? If not, it’s mostly noise.

My perspective from what I’ve seen across projects, only a few of these trends actually stick in a meaningful way:

AI — Real impact (but uneven): This is one of the few that’s genuinely changing day-to-day work—but not in the dramatic “replace everything” way people hype.

Cloud — Big impact, but mostly already absorbed: Cloud did fundamentally change things, but it’s no longer “new,” so people forget how big it was.

Clean Core (especially in SAP contexts) — Strategically important, operationally subtle: This one sound abstract, but it does matter—just not in flashy ways.

UX / Fiori / Design Thinking — Mixed impact: This one depends heavily on how seriously the organization takes it.

The real game changers (less hyped):

Interestingly, the biggest day-to-day differences often come from things that aren’t marketed as buzzwords:

Process standardization

  • Reduces confusion, rework, and exceptions
  • Makes systems easier to manage

Data quality improvements

  • Better decisions
  • Less firefighting

Automation (simple, not fancy)

  • RPA, scripts, workflows
  • Eliminates repetitive manual work

Cross-functional collaboration

  • Faster issue resolution
  • Better alignment between IT and business

So what really changes how we work?

If I had to boil it down:

  • AI → changing how fast you work
  • Cloud → changed how systems are built and deployed (already normalized)
  • Clean core → changing how disciplined you must be
  • UX → changing how easy systems are to use (sometimes)

But the biggest real-world impact usually comes from:

better processes + cleaner data + practical automation

entry.title: Measuring Business Outcomes in SAP Cloud ERP
entry.content:
This blog's intent is to expand on my previous one: Sample value drivers for effective Business Value Realization of your Public Cloud Solution.

Now that you have identified the value drivers and specific solution capabilities that can bring you additional business value, how do you go about knowing if you are improving? How do you go about measuring the value of your improvements without creating a sophisticated value driver baseline or measuring the value drivers in a qualitative way? In this blog, I also want to provide you with a simple and pragmatic approach using SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition standard applications as a source to get some information to establish the baseline (you might still need to do some calculations if you want to measure the exact value driver), but by using standard apps you can use them as an alternative to see if your business is improving, which, in the end, without a lot of effort, is what you want to do—measure improvement.  Obviously, if you want real value driver measurement you can also play with analytics in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to either create your own KPI, reports, multidimensional reports, stories, or booklets, to make an exact calculation of the value driver.

Remember, the approach I am suggesting here is not the only approach, as SAP has other services like Value Management and/or other tools like SAP Signavio Process Intelligence, etc., that can help you with this exercise as well. In my previous blog, I covered very briefly how SAP Signavio Process Intelligence — Drive more business outcomes out of your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition – A Recommendation Guide

Now let's recap on the different areas we suggested in my previous blog:

  • Reduce selling, general, and administrative expenses
  • Increase revenue
  • Decrease cost of goods sold (COGS)
  • Improve working capital
  • Improve pre-tax margin

Sample value drivers by improvement area

Selling, general, and administrative expenses

Ok, let’s start with lowering our selling, general, and administrative expenses. Here I recommended the following value drivers in my last blog: “Reduce account receivable management costs” and “Reduce invoice error rate.”

Reduce account receivable management costs – This value driver measures the total cost of accounts receivable as a percentage of revenue, so yes, you can use the system and create some specific analytics that can collect several general ledger accounts for your accounts receivable and then compare with your revenue to calculate this. So, somehow this value driver requires some information and calculation to come up with the total cost of the accounts receivable function and compare with your revenue. But my advice is that you instead look to see improvements metrics on accounts receivable to measure the real impact on improvements out of the box and not only the cost. For that, I recommend you use the Accounts Receivable Overview App, which will provide you with other KPIs to improve your AR function, like AR aging analysis, Top 10 Debtors, Exceeded Credit Limit, etc. This will provide you a meaningful measurement out of the box to measure improvements you can easily compare every time you use this app.

Accounts Receivables Overview

For the Reduce invoice error rate, as we are focusing on Accounts Receivable, I suggest you use the Overdue Receivables in Dispute and select some of the reasons like “Price Difference.” While this doesn’t give you an automatic error rate, you can use disputed amounts with the price difference reason, for example, to set the baseline to compare. Otherwise, you will need to create specific analytics that calculate all invoices and allow you to look at dispute cases over a specific time period, which complicates the measurement, unless you want to run it a bit more qualitatively.

Overdue Receivables in Dispute – filter by Reason: Price Difference

Revenue

On Revenue, we selected two different value drivers: Reduce manufacturing cycle time and Reduce revenue loss due to fulfillment issues.

Here I am recommending two apps that can provide you with this information. For reducing manufacturing cycle time, you can use the Production Execution Duration app. This app will be able to provide you with the plan and actual information by work center, which you can use to measure your improvements. You can then filter this by material, planning plant, production plan, and work center.

Production Execution Duration

Now for the Reduce revenue loss due to fulfillment issues, you can use the Sales Order Fulfillment – Analyze and Resolve app.

This app will help you look at current fulfillment issues and try to resolve them.

Sales Order Fulfillment – Analyze and Resolve

Cost of Good Sold

For the two selected value drivers, Reduce total manufacturing costs and Reduce asset maintenance costs, we have two apps that we suggest to use. The first one is the Analyze Production Costs – Event Based app, to help you measure the total manufacturing cost. There might be other apps you may want to use depending on your specific production process.

Production Costs – Event Based

The second one to help measure the reduction of maintenance costs is the app, Maintenance Order Costs Plan and Actuals. This app will help you to execute these tasks.

Maintenance Oder Costs – Plan/Actuals

Working Capital

In working capital, we suggested two value drivers: reduce sales days outstanding and reduce days in inventory.

To measure these two value drivers, you can use the following applications. For sales days outstanding, you can use the app “Days Sales Outstanding – 12 months,” which can provide you with a quick way to measure this value driver. The app provides you with the option to see this by period, by customer, and by company code.

Days Sales Outstanding – 12 months

The app Inventory Turnover Analysis can provide you with the reduction of days in inventory.

Inventory Turnover Analysis

Pre-tax Margin

In the pre-tax margin area, we selected two value drivers: improve on-time delivery performance and reduce uncollectible accounts receivable write-offs.

We could use the Delivery Performance app to help improve on-time delivery performance. We have two apps to choose from: Delivery Performance as Requested and Delivery Performance as Committed. Both apps look similar; the difference is one presents the information as requested and the other as committed. We also have an app that helps you analyze delivery performance – shipped as planned.

Delivery Performance as Requested

And to reduce uncollectible accounts receivable write-offs, we suggest using the app Cash Collector Tracker – Account Receivables. The app allows you to see this by Company Code, by Accounting Clerk, and by top 10 customers.

Collector Tracker – Account Receivables

I hope these out-of-the-box apps provide you an alternative to complex calculations to identify measurements for your value drivers as you drive improvements to your business.

I also want to thank my colleagues Edna Garcia and Jens Klawiter from Cloud ERP Product Success for the joint discussions on this topic and the checks we executed together in SAP Cloud ERP.

Keep in mind that solution capabilities are expanding from traditional functional scope to also include SAP Business AI, so SAP will continue to add more in this area. As in my previous blogs, remember, this is not the only tool that can be used; SAP has other tools such as SAP Signavio, SAP WalkMe, SAP LeanIX, and SAP Cloud ALM that can be used in tandem to help drive more business value from your SAP subscription.

I encourage you to discuss these points with your SAP Customer Success Manager, SAP Account Executive, or SAP Partner. They may be able to provide you with additional recommendations and guidance.

Your insights and opinions are valuable, so please don't hesitate to contribute by commenting on this blog.

Follow us via @SAP and #S4HANA or follow @Marco_Valencia (SAP Business Suite Customer Success Management).

entry.title: Re: Intent-based Navigation - semanticObjectAction annota...
entry.content:
Hi @RaminS @ I am probably too late and you might have already resolved this. What worked for me is putting all the properties in one element of UI.lineitem instead of putting 2 elements in it.

This works correctly and it opens a unique target directly without the popover

@UI           : {
      lineItem      : [{position: 10, importance: #HIGH, type: #WITH_INTENT_BASED_NAVIGATION, semanticObjectAction: 'display'}],
      selectionField: [{position: 70}]
    }

This doesnt work if you want a unique target and shows a popover

 @UI           : {
      lineItem      : [{position: 10, importance: #HIGH},{ type: #WITH_INTENT_BASED_NAVIGATION, semanticObjectAction: 'display'}],
      selectionField: [{position: 70}]
    }

  Probably this is why you were seeing 2 columns in one of your examples.

entry.title: Experiment with SAP RPT-1: Predicting the Right Carrier in SAP TM Using the RPT-1 API Token
entry.content:
In my previous blog about experimenting with RPT-1, we explored how to try out using the RPT-1 playground version. Now, let’s see how this can be done programmatically.  SAP Cloud SDK for Python not yet support RPT-1 natively. We will use request library to call the API directly.

Prerequisites:

  1. An API key for the service – you can get it from the ‘Your API Token’ section at https://rpt.cloud.sap/docs.

 

 

We will use the same .csv file used previously for the Freight Orders with ‘[PREDICT]’ tag.

RPT-1 API accept json format. So, lets first convert .csv file to json format.

#Read csv and convert to json
import csv
import json

def csv_to_json(csv_file_path):
    data = []

    with open(csv_file_path, encoding='utf-8') as csv_file:
        reader = csv.DictReader(csv_file)  
        for row in reader:
            data.append(row)

    
    final_payload = {
        "prediction_config": {
            "target_columns": [
                {
                    "name": "Carrier",
                    "prediction_placeholder": "[PREDICT]"
                }
            ]
        },
        "index_column": "Document" ,
        "rows": data          
    }
    print(f"Done! {len(data)} records converted to json")
    return final_payload

Prepare the request parameters

access_token = "<<Your access token here>>" #Use .env for secure storage

url = "https://rpt.cloud.sap/api/predict"

Now call the API

import requests



headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}",
    "Content-Type": "application/json",
    "Accept": "application/json",
}
final_payload = csv_to_json('sample.csv')

response = requests.post(url, headers=headers, json=final_payload, timeout=120)

response.raise_for_status()

Read result-

import json

data_pred = response.json()
#preds = data_pred["prediction"]
preds = data_pred.get("prediction", [])
print(json.dumps(preds, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False))

The result would look like this.

{
  "id": "ba15b9bd-8cde-4eee-b858-0329f1b48e11",
  "metadata": {
    "num_columns": 38,
    "num_predictions": 1,
    "num_query_rows": 1,
    "num_rows": 334
  },
  "predictions": [
    {
      "Carrier": [
        {
          "confidence": 0.46,
          "confidence_interval": null,
          "prediction": "MM_CAR_01"
        }
      ],
      "Document": 6100000600
    }
  ],
  "status": {
    "code": 0,
    "message": "ok"
  }
}

This example shows using Python, but this can be done in ABAP as well since this is an HTTP request call (such as a save process controller strategy method in SAP TM). The process is slightly different if you are using RPT-1 from AI Core Service and AI Launchpad (instead of API key, access token from AI Core needs to be passed in the request).

What you learned

  • How to shape tabular data for SAP RPT‑1 with a clear prediction_config and index_column
  • How to invoke the predict API securely and read confidence‑scored outputs
  • We used 'predicting carrier' just as an example and to understand the usage. You can think of any other use cases which require prediction from minimal data set which doesn't require complex classic ML training.

#SAPCommunity #SAPTM #SAPBTP #RPT1 #AI #Developers

entry.title: Re: Unable to find CDS view for MSME
entry.content:
Good day @Manish88 

Thank you for your question.

For your requirement, a CDS view is not the right object to make MSME number maintainable in the Maintain Business Partner app. For India certificate framework is the standard path, not to a supplier company code CDS field.

See the following references for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/e30de6eae4d24d70b65996ac8ff88848.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/60a09f68f2444ceca31dcac2e7017945/6156cdae3f5d4a8d9e86660e4ffa97ef.html?locale=en-US

If your goal is to maintain MSME in the app, use one of these two approaches:

  • Preferred standard approach is to use the certificate framework for supplier certificates in Business Partner, if your MSME requirement fits that standard object.
  • If the standard certificate object does not fit your requirement, then create a custom field in the Business Partner business context, not a CDS-only solution.

See the following references for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/60a09f68f2444ceca31dcac2e7017945/2929a28bddb84f2ca5b0804967f604b4.html?locale=en-US

Maintaining Details of Suppliers' Certificates | SAP Help Portal

However, if your goal is reporting or you want to extract supplier company code data around that filed, then for a custom CDS view, the best starting point is typically to use released I_SUPPLIERCOMPANY. You can combine it with other released BP-related sources if you need.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: How to set up OUTPUT parameters for PO to send Email only to Goods Supplier(WS) & not to Vendor (LF)
entry.content:
we are trying to send Purchase order to as Email only to Goods Supplier(WS) & not to Vendor (LF) 

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to set up OUTPUT parameters for PO to send Email...
entry.content:
Hi,

You may refer to Output Parameter Determination | SAP Help Portal

This explains how Output Parameter Determination works in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, including the key steps of Receiver, Channel, and Email Recipient determination.

To achieve the desired outcome, please ensure the following:

  1. Maintain partner function WS (Goods Supplier) in all relevant Purchase Orders (either Business Partner master data or partner determination).
  2. In Output Parameter Determination, maintain the rule with Role = WS and Channel = EMAIL, and set the Exclusive Indicator.
  3. Remove or restrict any rules defined for Role = LF (Vendor) with Channel = EMAIL to prevent unintended output.
  4. Optionally, add a condition to ensure the rule is only evaluated when WS is available.

Please note that if WS is not maintained in the Purchase Order, no output will be triggered when using this setup.

You may also refer to KBA 3419427 - Send Purchase Order emails to supplier email address maintained in business partner master data - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

Thanks and regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Intermittent Non-Delivery of Emails from “Schedule Bi...
entry.content:
Good day @Toms_Lopes 

Thank you for your question.

Question: Is there anything that could be blocking these messages?

Answer: Yes, if the billing output item is completed and the email status is sent, but the customer still does not receive the email. There are two main causes to check first:

  1. The SPF record for the sender domain is not published, and or
  2. You have not set up DKIM properly.

So, a status of sent in the system in the system does not by itself guarantee final delivery to the recipient. The message may still be filtered or rejected after SAP has handed it over for outbound email processing, especially if SPF or DKIM is missing or incorrect. See KBA 3274509 for more details.

Also check the following configuration in your system:

  • Confirm if a recipient was determined
  • Confirm the sender domain is allowed
  • Confirm the receiver address is allowed in your test system.

Question: does the system validate whether the email addresses are valid?

Anwer: It partially does. The system validates certain technical and configuration aspects. See the previous checks you are to carry out. Those are valid details.

Note that recipients can come from the related Output Parameter Determination or from the related BP master data when the default email address is used. If the system does not find any recipient, then it will raise an error.

See the following notes KBA 2767106 , 2676588 , 2667346 , 3631820  for more insights.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Make a synchronous response from a SAP DM PPD from a Core plugin extension
entry.content:
Hi,

My use case is as below:

I am building a core plugin extension of worklist plugin and I need to show a custom field related to order from SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. I have built a custom OData api in S/4HANA Public cloud. The S/4HANA api is added in 'Manage Web Server' app and then the service url is registered in 'Manage Service Registry' app.

Created a production process to call above service and published it to service service registry.

I am calling the production process in CreateExtension.js of the worklist plugin extension controller. The HTTP call is working fine and I can see the response in network tab of developer's console of the browser.

My analysis is that standard function 'ajaxPostRequest' of sap.dm.dme.podfoundation.controller.PluginViewController makes an asynchronous call by default and hence the formatter function cannot return any value. I am directly uploading the plugin in SAP DM and not using MTA deployment in BTP approach (i.e., I am using POD 1.1 approach).

Following is the code I am using in 

getColumnConfiguration: function (aColumnConfiguration) {
            let that = this;
            let oTextControl = new Text().bindProperty("text", {
                parts: [
                    { path: "shopOrder", type: new sap.ui.model.type.String() }
                ],
                formatter: function (sOrder) {
                    let returnValue = '';
                    let sUrl = this.getController().getPeRestDataSourceUri() + 'api/v1/process/processDefinitions/start?key=REG_88f81c18-1bbf-4357-94f5-94e430665091&async=false';
                    const oPayload = {
                        "ProductionOrder": sOrder
                    };
                    this.getController().ajaxPostRequest(sUrl, oPayload,
                        function (oResponseData) {
                            if (oResponseData.DFHAttributes) {
                                returnValue = oResponseData.DFHAttributes;
                            }
                        },
                        function (oError, sHttpErrorMessage) {
                            let err = oError || sHttpErrorMessage;
                            that._oExtensionUtilities.logMessage("Unable to get Custom Data Value");
                            returnValue = 'ERROR';
                        });
                    

                    return returnValue;
                }.bind(this),
                wrapping: true
            });

            aColumnConfiguration.push({
                columnId: 'CUSTOM_DATA',
                description: 'Custom Data',
                hAlign: "Begin",
                vAlign: "Middle",
                wrapping: false,
                width: "15em",
                label: "Custom Data",
                // binding: "{sfc}",
                columnListItem: oTextControl
            });

            return aColumnConfiguration;
        },

I have seen sample in the orderListExtensionProvider where it has created its own function _ajaxSyncGetRequest(). But having to do this in my case of POST request, it is too much of heavy lifting and also the endpoints may differ in different subaccounts of the landscape (qa vs prod etc.) (Can we use BTP destination?). 

1. If my understanding is correct. then how can I make this work so that the formatter function as above can return the custom field value?

2. Or is there any other better way/design to achieve this?

@ManoelCosta @Kevin_Hunter1 @tobias_renth 

Best regards,

Sumit

entry.title: Compensation salary - Mandatory Compensation Adjustments with Approval Workflow & Multiple Launches
entry.content:
Hi experts,

I’m currently working with a client who has a specific requirement around mandatory compensation adjustments.

Initially, we considered processing these changes directly through Employee Central. However, the client would like these adjustments, despite being mandatory, to go through an approval workflow, specifically requiring approval from a higher-level role (e.g., CEO).

To support this, we are exploring the use of Compensation Worksheets.

That said, there are a couple of challenges:

The adjustments are mandatory (not discretionary)
The client wants to trigger this process multiple times throughout the year (not just during a standard annual cycle)
From a design perspective, this raises some concerns around usability, administration, and system limitations.

My questions are:

Have you implemented a similar requirement using Compensation Worksheets?
How did you handle multiple launches during the year? (e.g., multiple templates vs. re-launch strategy)
Are there alternative approaches you would recommend (e.g., EC workflows, MDF-based solutions, or hybrid approaches)?
Any insights, best practices, or lessons learned would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance!
SAP SuccessFactors Compensation 
@xavierlegarrec

entry.title: Re: Transport Calendar
entry.content:
Dear @FredericoBraga,

For Public Cloud SAP documented route for Factory Calendar is not the CBC / Export Customizing Transports path. SAP says Factory Calendar transport is a manual step performed by the Key User Administrator and the calendar entries are automatically added to the default software collection CALENDAR (Factory and Holiday Calendar Collection) in Export Software Collection. SAP Note then gives the two-step flow: export in Q and import in P using Import Collection

So the supported process is:

  1. Maintain the holiday / factory calendar in the source tenant
  2. Open Export Software Collection app
  3. Use the default collection CALENDAR
  4. Export it
  5. In the target tenant, use Import Collection to import it

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3195499

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3482742

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F1433')/S36

The important distinction is that SAP separates transport categories in Public Cloud. The central transport Note shows that business configuration uses Export Customizing Transports, while software collections are exported with Export Software Collection and imported with Import Collection. Factory Calendar is documented under the software collection route so this should be handled through CALENDAR, not through CBC customizing transport export

IMPORTANT: If you are not able to export at all, SAP also documents cases where Key User Extensibility Transports are inactive because no target test system is available yet, or because the tenant is a Starter / Sales Demo tenant where transport is not supported

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3485987

Summary:

In Public Cloud Factory Calendar is moved via the default software collection CALENDAR using Export Software Collection and Import Collection. It is not handled via the normal CBC / Export Customizing Transports route

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: requirement dates in capacity scheduling table
entry.content:
Dear @Jana_Hauck,

In Public Cloud the 00:00 shown in the Requirement Date field of the Capacity Scheduling Table should be treated as standard behavior for this scenario. SAP documents that scheduling agreement schedule lines can contain a delivery time (ScheduleLineDeliveryTime) but the Capacity Scheduling Table is documented around capacity scheduling and dynamic pegging requirement date logic and SAP does not document a standard setting or released extensibility option to derive or map the scheduling agreement release time into that Requirement Date field. The released app extensibility documented for Capacity Scheduling Table is focused on areas such as the calculation of Latest Start Date and Latest End Date, not on remapping the displayed requirement time from EDI schedule lines. Therefore the time cannot be influenced in standard for this field and for this specific field and scenario the requirement time is represented at date level, which is why the time is displayed as 00:00

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/6e52f71dfb5a481b810ce0697708baf2.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/8d2e44f0c279408ba2790e9ad0ac5188.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/1323c8d9c7ac4ee685d3d25c5fec33f1.html

At the same time this should not be interpreted as meaning that planning in Public Cloud is generally performed only on a daily basis. SAP documents that relevant capacity planning apps calculate date and time based on the plant time zone, so the broader planning framework is not universally restricted to date-only logic. The limitation here is specific to how the Requirement Date is represented in this app scenario, not a general Public Cloud rule that all planning is day-based only. If the business needs the exact EDI delivery time the recommended approach is to use the scheduling agreement release / schedule line data itself or a custom analytical report based on the schedule line delivery time field, rather than the Requirement Date shown in Capacity Scheduling Table

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Conversational AI vs Generative AI: What’s the Difference?
entry.content:
Overview:

Recently, everywhere there is a lot of buzz or discussion about booming out the AI revolution. Artificial Intelligence has been evolving continuously as well as rapidly.

This blog could be basic content which will help us to get a clear understanding about the actual difference between Conversational AI vs Generative AI.

While both terms may seem similar, but they actually serve a different purpose and built on different capabilities.

 

Conversational AI:

Conversational AI is nothing but a conversation that happens between machines and human. In other words, we can say, AI focuses on enabling machines to interact with us (humans) through natural language in a structured way.

One of the popular examples is SAP Conversational AI. It helps businesses to create chatbots and virtual assistants.

In one-line, it responds based on trained intents and workflows, but not true creativity.

In simple words, we can describe it as task-oriented (e.g: booking, support, FAQs).

Example: We have likely come across chatbots such as the following: Answers HR queries, helps reset passwords, Tracks order status.

In other hand Generative AI means,

Generative AI (Gen AI) goes beyond simple conversation, it can create entirely new content like text, images, code, stories or even music too. One of the most well-known examples is ChatGPT. 

 In one-line, we can say, it creates new content, not only just selects from predefined answers.

Example: Most of us already using these Gen AI for day-to-day activities, such as  generating emails, summarizing documents and analysis the best products on e-commerce sites, so on.

 

Key Features:

Conversational AIGenerative AI (Gen AI)

Predefined or trained intents and responses

Content creation (text, images, code)

Context-aware conversations

Contextual understanding with creativity

Work via chat or voice interfaces

Learns patterns from large datasets and provides open-ended responses

 

Technologies Used:

Conversational AIGenerative AI (Gen AI)

Natural Language Processing(helps humans to communicate with systems naturally)

Large Language Models (AI systems designed to understand & interact using human language)

Rule-based systems + Machine Learning (ML)

Deep Learning (DL - multi-layered neural networks)

 

Key Differences:

Feature

Conversational AI

Generative AI

Purpose

Interaction

Content creation

Responses

Predefined/Structured

Dynamic/Creative

Flexibility

Limited

Highly Flexible

Training

Intent-based

Large-scale data training

Example

Chatbots

Image generators

In modern systems (including SAP ecosystems), both are often working together: Conversational AI handles user interaction, Generative AI enhances responses with dynamic content

Example: A chatbot powered by Conversational AI can use Generative AI to generate more natural and also human-like answers.

 

As end of the blog, the conclusion of these two terminologies as follows: 

Conversational AI means talking to machines (structured) whereas Generative AI means creating new content (creative).

Both are powerful and together they form the foundation of next-generation intelligent applications.

Thank you for taking the time to read this blog! If you found this helpful, i would love to hear your thoughts, feedback or questions in the comments. Let's keep learning and growing together!

I’m still new to blogging, so if you notice anything that could be improved or corrected, please don’t hesitate to let me know!!

entry.title: Re: Cannot select a system in BAS when creating an Adapta...
entry.content:
Dear @chrisobar,

For Public Cloud a Basic Authentication destination is not the supported setup for SAP Business Application Studio integration / SAPUI5 Adaptation Project. SAP current Public Cloud guidance is explicit for BAS integration with S/4HANA Cloud, only SAMLAssertion is allowed. SAP also separately clarifies that Basic Authentication belongs to the external API scenario, which is not intended for building UIs. That also explains why your curl test can succeed while the Adaptation Project wizard still fails. SAP has a Note for the exact pattern where the destination works from BAS terminal commands but the Adaptation Project still returns 403 / authentication-related failure. In other words, successful curl only proves connectivity with the credentials you passed it does not validate that the destination is correct for the BAS adaptation flow

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3445942

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3297481

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003225635

So the correction is:

1. Replace the destination authentication type with SAMLAssertion. Do not continue with BasicAuthentication for this scenario. That is the primary issue based on SAP documented setup for BAS + Public Cloud

2. If the trust is email-based, validate the Name ID format in the destination. SAP setup guide specifies urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.1:nameid-format:emailAddress when the subaccount sends the e-mail address as the SAML Subject Name Identifier

More details below:

https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/abap-custom-ui-bas-connect-s4hc..html

3. Verify the communication system / SAML bearer certificate in the development tenant. SAP documents that an expired certificate in the S/4HANA Cloud communication system can cause this BAS authentication failure and the fix is to download the trust again from BTP and upload the renewed certificate in S/4HANA Cloud

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3582104

4. Make sure you are selecting the development tenant destination, not another tenant and that your user has a role based on SAP_BR_DEVELOPER with the required developer catalogs. SAP lists this as a prerequisite for adaptation projects in Public Cloud

5. Also verify that the target app is released for adaptation. SAP states that only released applications can be extended with Adaptation Projects in this scenario

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_FIORI_tools/17d50220bcd848aa854c9c182d65b699/f4881a9b9141430dba1c6a76e7f456dc.html

Summary:

This is not evidence that the S/4 credentials are wrong. The more likely issue is that the destination is not configured in the supported way for this BAS adaptation scenario. The current destination type is not the supported one for SAPUI5 Adaptation Project in Public Cloud. For this scenario the destination must be configured with SAMLAssertion, not Basic Authentication. After that, validate nameIdFormat if email-based trust is used, refresh the communication system certificate if needed and confirm the development-tenant/authorization prerequisites

Best Regards,

Dawid
